GS3.trivia: The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960, aims to prevent the infliction of unnecessary pain or suffering on animals.

GS3.headline: India Records Unprecedented Growth in Digital Payments, UPI Leads the Way
GS3.explainer:
• A new report by the National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) highlighted a phenomenal surge in digital payment transactions, with UPI (Unified Payments Interface) emerging as the dominant platform.
• The volume and value of UPI transactions have consistently broken records, reflecting widespread adoption across urban and rural demographics.
• Factors contributing to this growth include government initiatives like Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile (JAM) trinity, demonetization, and the rapid expansion of smartphone penetration.
• The report also noted significant growth in other digital payment methods such as IMPS, net banking, and mobile wallets, contributing to financial inclusion.
• This shift towards a cashless economy is transforming consumer behavior and boosting formal economic activity, but challenges related to cybersecurity and digital literacy persist.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: UPI launched in 2016.
Developed by NPCI.
BHIM app is built on UPI.
NPCI is an umbrella organization for operating retail payments and settlement systems in India.
RBI regulates digital payment systems.
mainsPoints: Examine the role of UPI and other digital payment platforms in fostering financial inclusion and transforming India's economy.
Discuss the benefits and challenges associated with the rapid expansion of digital payments, including issues of cybersecurity, digital literacy, and infrastructure.
Analyze how government policies and technological innovations have driven the growth of digital payments and their implications for future economic development.
GS3.imageDescription: A person making a payment using a smartphone with a UPI app.
GS3.trivia: India's UPI system is being adopted by several other countries, including Singapore, France, and UAE, for cross-border transactions.

GS3.headline: India to Deploy Advanced Indigenous Anti-Drone Systems Along Borders
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence announced plans to deploy indigenously developed advanced anti-drone systems along India's western and northern borders to counter increasing threats from unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).
• These systems, developed by DRDO and private sector partners, incorporate AI-based detection, jamming, and soft-kill/hard-kill capabilities to neutralize hostile drones.
• The move comes in response to rising incidents of drone intrusions for smuggling of arms, drugs, and reconnaissance, posing significant security challenges.
• The deployment aims to enhance border security, protect critical infrastructure, and strengthen India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative in defence technology.
• Extensive trials have validated the effectiveness of these systems in diverse operational environments.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Anti-drone systems developed by DRDO.
Deployment along western and northern borders.
UAVs pose threats like smuggling, espionage.
'Atmanirbhar Bharat' in defence aims for self-reliance.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of drone warfare and the security challenges posed by UAVs along India's borders and in internal security scenarios.
Analyze the significance of indigenous development and deployment of anti-drone systems in enhancing India's border security and strategic autonomy.
Examine the technological capabilities of these systems and the need for a multi-pronged approach to counter drone threats effectively.
GS3.imageDescription: An anti-drone system detecting and neutralizing a drone.
GS3.trivia: Drones are increasingly being used in modern warfare for surveillance, reconnaissance, and targeted strikes, necessitating robust counter-drone measures.

GS3.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 Focuses on Circular Economy in Waste Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA) highlighted the progress of Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 (SBM-U 2.0), emphasizing its shift towards a circular economy model in waste management.
• The mission aims to achieve 'Garbage-Free Cities' by focusing on 100% source segregation, material recovery facilities, and waste-to-wealth initiatives.
• Key interventions include promoting composting, recycling, and scientific processing of all waste streams, minimizing landfill dependence.
• Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are being empowered with technical and financial support to implement integrated solid waste management systems.
• This approach not only addresses environmental concerns but also creates economic opportunities and promotes sustainable urban development.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
GS3.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA)
prelimsPoints: SBM-U 2.0 launched in 2021.
Goal: 'Garbage Free Cities' by 2026.
Focus on solid waste management and used water management.
Comes under MoHUA.
mainsPoints: Discuss the paradigm shift towards a circular economy in waste management under Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 and its implications for sustainable urban development.
Analyze the challenges faced by Urban Local Bodies in implementing scientific waste management practices and achieving 100% source segregation.
Suggest innovative solutions and policy measures to foster public participation and integrate informal waste pickers into the formal waste management system.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing different stages of waste segregation and recycling.
GS3.trivia: India generates approximately 1.5 lakh metric tonnes of municipal solid waste per day.

GS3.headline: Remembering Valmik Thapar: A Legacy of Wildlife Conservation and Tiger Protection
GS3.explainer:
• Valmik Thapar, a renowned Indian naturalist, author, and conservationist, is celebrated for his relentless dedication to wildlife, especially tigers.
• His extensive work through books, documentaries, and advocacy has significantly contributed to raising awareness about tiger conservation and the challenges faced by India's wildlife.
• Thapar has been instrumental in shaping wildlife policies and promoting scientific research on tiger behavior and ecology, particularly in Ranthambore National Park.
• His writings often highlight the intricate relationship between humans and nature, advocating for coexistence and sustainable conservation strategies.
• His legacy continues to inspire a new generation of environmentalists and policy-makers to protect India's rich biodiversity.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity and its conservation.
prelimsPoints: Valmik Thapar is a celebrated Indian naturalist.
Known for work on tiger conservation.
Associated with Ranthambore National Park.
Authored several books on wildlife.
mainsPoints: Discuss the contributions of individuals like Valmik Thapar in shaping India's wildlife conservation movement and public awareness about biodiversity.
Analyze the challenges in tiger conservation in India and the multi-faceted approach required, including habitat protection, anti-poaching measures, and community involvement.
Examine the role of naturalists and researchers in influencing environmental policy and promoting sustainable practices for human-wildlife coexistence.
GS3.imageDescription: A photograph of Valmik Thapar observing a tiger in the wild.
GS3.trivia: Ranthambore National Park in Rajasthan is one of India's largest and most famous tiger reserves.

GS3.headline: Government Introduces New Policy to Accelerate Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Power unveiled a revised policy framework aimed at significantly boosting the deployment of Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure across India.
• The policy simplifies the licensing process for charging stations, offers subsidies for setting up public charging points, and encourages private investment in the sector.
• It mandates the use of renewable energy sources for charging stations wherever feasible, promoting green mobility and reducing carbon footprint.
• The goal is to create a robust and widespread charging network to address range anxiety, a major barrier to EV adoption, and support India's ambitious EV targets.
• This move is expected to accelerate the transition to electric mobility, reduce dependence on fossil fuels, and combat urban air pollution.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation.
prelimsPoints: New policy by Ministry of Power.
Aims to boost EV charging infrastructure.
Encourages renewable energy use for charging.
India aims for 30% EV penetration by 2030.
mainsPoints: Discuss the critical role of robust charging infrastructure in accelerating the adoption of Electric Vehicles in India and achieving its clean energy goals.
Analyze the key features of the new EV charging policy and its potential impact on overcoming barriers to EV adoption, such as range anxiety and high upfront costs.
Examine the environmental and economic benefits of transitioning to electric mobility and the challenges in ensuring a sustainable and equitable energy transition.
GS3.imageDescription: An electric car being charged at a public charging station.
GS3.trivia: India's first EV charging station was inaugurated in Nagpur in 2017.

GS3.headline: Government Launches 'Bharat Shree Anna' Scheme to Promote Millet Consumption and Production
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched the 'Bharat Shree Anna' scheme, a new initiative to boost the production, processing, and consumption of millets across India.
• The scheme provides financial incentives to farmers for millet cultivation, supports the establishment of millet processing units, and promotes millets as 'Shree Anna' (super food) in public distribution systems and mid-day meals.
• It aims to enhance food and nutritional security, diversify cropping patterns, and support climate-resilient agriculture, given millets' ability to thrive in arid conditions.
• Awareness campaigns will be conducted to highlight the health benefits of millets and integrate them into daily diets.
• This initiative aligns with the International Year of Millets 2023 and India's efforts to become a global hub for millet production.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security.
prelimsPoints: Bharat Shree Anna scheme launched by Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare.
Aims to promote millets (Shree Anna).
India championed International Year of Millets 2023.
Millets are climate-resilient crops.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of millets as 'Shree Anna' in addressing India's food and nutritional security challenges and promoting climate-resilient agriculture.
Analyze the key features of the 'Bharat Shree Anna' scheme and its potential impact on farmers' income, dietary diversification, and public health.
Examine the challenges in mainstreaming millet consumption and production in India and suggest measures to overcome them, including market linkages and value addition.
GS3.imageDescription: Various types of millets displayed in bowls.
GS3.trivia: India is the largest producer of millets in the world, accounting for about 41% of global production.
